concepts.title: Binomial Expression
concepts.conceptBody:
Binomial Expression

An algebraic expression consisting of two terms with a positive or negative sign between them is called a binomial expression.

Example : $\left( \text{a}+\text{b} \right),\left( \text{P}/{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)-\left( \text{Q}/{{\text{x}}^{4}} \right)$ etc.

Binomial Theorem: 

When a binomial expression is raised to a power ' $n$ ' we would like to be able to expand it. The binomial theorem assists us in doing this. It converts such an expression into a series.

Binomial Theorem for positive integral index: ${{(\text{x}+\text{y})}^{\text{n}}}={{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-1}}\text{y}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-2}}{{\text{y}}^{2}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots \cdot {{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}{{\text{y}}^{\text{r}}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots +\ldots \ldots \ldots +{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}-1}}\text{x}{{\text{y}}^{\text{n}-1}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{c}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{y}}^{\text{n}}}.$

Proof of Binomial Theorem: 

The Binomial theorem can be proved by mathematical induction

Let $\text{P}\left( \text{n} \right)$ stands for the mathematical statement

${{(\text{x}+\text{a})}^{\text{n}}}={{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-1}}\text{a}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-2}}{{\text{a}}^{2}}+\ldots \text{n}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}{{\text{a}}^{\text{r}}}+\ldots +{{\text{a}}^{\text{r}}}\ldots \ldots $ (i)

Note that there are $\left( n+1 \right)$ terms in R.H.S. and all the terms are of the same degree in $x$ and a together

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}\left( 1 \right)$ is verified to be true

Assume $\text{P}\left( \text{m} \right)$ to be true

i.e., ${{(x+a)}^{m}}={{x}^{m}}+{{~}^{m}}{{C}_{1}}{{x}^{m-1}}a+{{~}^{m}}{{C}_{2}}{{x}^{m-2}}{{a}^{2}}+\ldots {{~}^{m}}{{C}_{r}}{{x}^{m-r}}{{a}^{r}}+\ldots +{{a}^{m}}\ldots \ldots $.

Multiplying equation (ii) by $\left( x+a \right)$, we have

${{(\text{x}+\text{a})}^{\text{m}}}\left( \text{x}+\text{a} \right)=\left( \text{x}+\text{a} \right)\left\{ {{\text{x}}^{\text{m}}}+\text{m}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{m}-1}}\text{a}+{{~}^{\text{m}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{m}-2}}{{\text{a}}^{2}}+\ldots +{{~}^{\text{m}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{m}-\text{r}}}{{\text{a}}^{\text{r}}}+\ldots +{{\text{a}}^{\text{m}}} \right\}$

i.e. ${{(x+a)}^{m+1}}={{x}^{m+1}}+\left( {{~}^{m}}{{C}_{1}}+1 \right){{x}^{m}}a+\left( {{~}^{m}}{{C}_{2}}+{{~}^{m}}{{C}_{1}} \right){{x}^{m-1}}{{a}^{2}}+\ldots +\left( {{~}^{m}}{{C}_{r}}+{{~}^{m}}{{C}_{r-1}} \right){{x}^{m-r}}+1{{a}^{r}}+\ldots {{a}^{m+1}}$ $={{\text{x}}^{\text{m}+1}}+{{~}^{\left( \text{m}+1 \right)}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{m}}}\text{a}+{{~}^{\left( \text{m}+1 \right)}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{m}-1}}{{\text{a}}^{2}}+\ldots $

${{~}^{\text{m}+1}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{m}+1-\text{r}}}{{\text{a}}^{\text{r}}}+{{\text{a}}^{\text{m}+1}}\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots $ (iii)

(using the formula ${{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}-1}}={{~}^{\left( \text{n}+1 \right)}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}$ )

Equation (iii) implies that $\text{P}\left( \text{m}+1 \right)$ is true and hence by induction $\text{P}\left( \text{n} \right)$ is true.

Particular - Cases: 

(i) Replacing ' $y$ ' by ' $-\text{y}$ ', we have :

${{(\text{x}-\text{y})}^{\text{n}}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}\text{xy}{{~}^{0}}-{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-1}}\text{y}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-2}}{{\text{y}}^{2}}\ldots \ldots +\left( -1 \right){{~}^{\text{rn}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}{{\text{y}}^{\text{r}}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots {{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{0}}{{\text{y}}^{\text{n}}}.$

It can be represented as:

\((x-y)^n=\sum^n{ }_{r=0}(-1)^r{ }^n C_r x^{n-r} y^r\)

(ii) Replacing ' $\text{x}$ ' by ' 1 ' and ' $y$ ' by ' $\text{x}$ ', we have :

${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}{{\text{x}}^{0}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{x}+\text{n}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots +{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots +{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}-1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-1}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}$.

or ${{(1+x)}^{n}}=\sum _{r=0}^{n}{{~}^{n}}{{C}_{r}}{{x}^{r}}$

(ii) Replacing ' $\text{x}$ ' by ' $-\text{x}$ ', we have :

${{(1-\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}{{\text{x}}^{0}}-{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{1}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}-\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots +{{(-1)}^{\text{r}}}{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots ..{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}-1}}{{(-1)}^{\text{n}-1}}+{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}$

or ${{(1-x)}^{n}}=\sum _{r=0}^{n}{{(-1)}^{r}}{{~}^{n}}{{C}_{r}}{{x}^{r}}$

concepts.title: General term
concepts.conceptBody:
General terms

$\left( \text{r}+1 \right)$ th term from beginning in ${{(\text{x}+\text{y})}^{\text{n}}}$ is called general - term, and it is denoted by ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}{{\text{y}}^{\text{r}}}$

Explanation: We know

(x $y{{)}^{\text{n}}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{y}}^{0}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-1}}{{\text{y}}^{1}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-2}}{{\text{y}}^{2}}+$ ${{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{0}}{{\text{y}}^{0}}$

Here:

First term $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{1}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{y}}^{0}}$

${{\text{T}}_{2}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-1}}{{\text{y}}^{1}}$

${{\text{T}}_{3}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-2}}{{\text{y}}^{2}}$

...................................

....................................

${{\text{T}}_{r}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{r-1}}\text{ }{{\text{x}}^{n\text{ }\!\!\_\!\!\text{ }(r-1)}}{{\text{y}}^{r-1}}$

Putting $r=r+1$ in this expression, we get;

General term : ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}{{\text{y}}^{\text{r}}}$

Note : ' ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}$ ' can be used as general terms also.

Problems based on general terms 

Type 1)  Find the ${{7}^{th~}}$ term in expansion of ${{[4x-\left( 1/2\sqrt{x} \right)]}^{13}}$ 

Solution : We know the general term is ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}\And $ we want to find the ${{7}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term. $\therefore \text{r}+1=7\Rightarrow \text{r}=6$

${{\text{T}}_{7}}$$~={{\text{T}}_{6+1}}={{~}^{13}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{(4\text{x})}^{13-6}}-{{(1/2\sqrt{\text{x}})}^{6}}$

$~={{~}^{13}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}\cdot {{4}^{7}}{{\text{x}}^{7}}\cdot 1/\left( {{2}^{6}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)$

$~={{~}^{13}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}\cdot {{2}^{8}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{4}}$

$~=13!/\left( 6!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }7! \right)\cdot {{2}^{8}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{4}}$

$~={{\text{T}}_{7}}=439296{{\text{x}}^{4}}$

Type 2) Find the coefficient of ${{X}^{-7}}$ in the expansion of ${{\left( ax-1/b{{x}^{2}} \right)}^{11}}$ 

Solution 

General term, ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{~}^{11}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{(\text{ax})}^{11-\text{r}}}-{{\left( \frac{-1}{\text{b}{{\text{x}}^{2}}} \right)}^{\text{r}}}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{(-1)}^{\text{r}}}{{~}^{11}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\left( \frac{{{\text{a}}^{11-\text{r}}}}{{{\text{b}}^{\text{r}}}} \right)\frac{{{\text{x}}^{11-\text{r}}}}{{{\text{x}}^{2\text{r}}}} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow {{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{~}^{11}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\left( \frac{{{\text{a}}^{11-\text{r}}}}{{{\text{b}}^{\text{r}}}} \right){{\text{x}}^{11-3\text{r}}}\ldots ...(i) \\ \end{array}$

Since we want to find the coefficient of ${{\text{x}}^{-7}}$

Putting $11-3\text{r}=-7$

$\Rightarrow \text{r}=6$

From (i) to ${{\text{T}}_{7}}={{(-1)}^{6}}{{~}^{11}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}\cdot \left( {{\text{a}}^{5}}/{{\text{b}}^{6}} \right){{\text{x}}^{-7}}$…………. (i)

Hence, the coefficient of ${{\text{x}}^{-7}}$ is ${{~}^{11}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{a}}^{5}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}^{-6}}$.

Type 3) Find the term independent of ' x ' in ${{\left[ \left( 3{{x}^{2}}/2 \right)-\left( 1/3x \right) \right]}^{9}}$ 

Solution : General term, ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\left( 3{{\text{x}}^{2}}/2 \right)}^{9-\text{r}}}-{{(1/3\text{x})}^{\text{r}}}$

$={{(-1)}^{\text{r}}}{{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\left( 3/2 \right){{~}^{9-\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{18-2\text{r}}}\left( 1/{{3}^{\text{r}}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}} \right)$

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{(-1)}^{\text{r}9}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\left( {{3}^{9-2\text{r}}}/{{2}^{9-\text{r}}} \right)\cdot {{\text{x}}^{18-3\text{r}}}$

Putting $18-3\text{r}=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ r}=6$

So, from (i), ${{7}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term is independent of ' $x$ ' and its value is :

${{\text{T}}_{7}}={{(-1)}^{6}}\cdot {{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}\cdot \left( {{3}^{-3}}/{{2}^{3}} \right){{\text{x}}^{0}}$

$=9!/\left( 6!3! \right)\cdot 1/\left( \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{3}^{3}} & {{2}^{3}} \\ \end{array} \right)$

$={{\text{T}}_{7}}=\left( 7/18 \right)$

$\text{p}$ th term form end: 

' $p$ 'th term from end in the expansion of ${{(x+y)}^{n}}$ is ${{(n-p+2)}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term from beginning.

Type 4) Find the ${{4}^{th~}}$ term from the end in the expansion of \({{\left[ \left( {{x}^{3}}/2 \right)-\left( 2/{{x}^{2}} \right) \right]}^{7}}\) 

Solution: 

${{4}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term from end $={{(7-4+2)}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ or ${{5}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term from beginning.

${{\text{T}}_{5}}={{\text{T}}_{4+1}}={{~}^{7}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}{{\left( {{\text{x}}^{3}}/2 \right)}^{7-4}}\cdot {{\left( -2/{{\text{x}}^{4}} \right)}^{4}}$

$={{~}^{7}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}{{\left( {{\text{x}}^{3}}/2 \right)}^{3}}{{\left( -2/{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)}^{4}}$

$=7!/\left( 4!3! \right)\cdot \left( {{x}^{9}}/8 \right)\cdot \left( 16/{{x}^{8}} \right)$

$=\left( 7.6.5/3.2.1 \right).2\text{x}$

${{\text{T}}_{5}}=70\text{x}$

Hence ' 4 ' term, from the end $=70\text{x}$.

concepts.title: Middle term
concepts.conceptBody:
Middle terms

It depends upon the value of ' $n$ '

Case 1 : When ' $n$ ' is even, then total number of terms in $\left( x+y \right)n$ is odd. so there is only one middle term i.e. $\left[ \left( \text{n}/2 \right)+1 \right]$ th them in the middle term.

So we find $\left( {{T}_{n+1/2}} \right)$. th term is this case, if ' $n$ ' is even.

Case II : When ' $n$ ' is odd, then the total number of terms in ${{(x+y)}^{n}}$ is even. So there are two middle terms i.e. $\left( \text{n}+1 \right)1/2$ th and $\left( \frac{\text{n}+3}{2} \right)$ th are true middle terms.

So we find ${{\text{T}}_{\left( \text{n}+1 \right)/2}}$ th and ${{\text{T}}_{\left( \text{n}+3 \right)/2}}$ th in this case if ' $\text{n}$ ' is odd.

Find the middle term in the expansion of ${{\left[ 3x-\left( {{x}^{3}}/6 \right) \right]}^{9}}$ 

Solution: 

Here total no. of terms are 10 (even). So there re true middle terms.

i.e. $\left( 9+1 \right)/2$ th and $\left( 9+3 \right)/2$ th. So we have to find out ' ${{\text{T}}_{5}}$ ' and ' ${{\text{T}}_{6}}$ '.

${{\text{T}}_{5}}={{\text{T}}_{4+1}}={{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}{{(3\text{x})}^{9-4}}{{\left( -{{\text{x}}^{3}}/6 \right)}^{4}}$

$=9!/\left( 4!5! \right)\cdot {{3}^{5}}{{x}^{5}}\left( {{x}^{12}}/{{6}^{4}} \right)$

$=\left( 9.8.7.6/4.3.2.1 \right).35\left( {{2}^{4}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{3}^{4}} \right){{\text{x}}^{17}}$

${{\text{T}}_{5}}=\left( 189/8 \right){{\text{x}}^{17}}$

${{\text{T}}_{6}}={{\text{T}}_{5+1}}={{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{5}}{{(3\text{x})}^{9-5}}{{\left( -{{\text{x}}^{3}}/6 \right)}^{5}}$

$=9!/\left( 5!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }4! \right)\cdot {{3}^{4}}{{\text{x}}^{4}}\left( {{\text{x}}^{15}}/{{6}^{5}} \right)$

$=-\left( 9.8.7.6/4.3.2.1 \right){{3}^{4}}\left( {{2}^{5}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{3}^{5}} \right){{\text{x}}^{19}}$

${{\text{T}}_{6}}=-\left( 21/16 \right){{\text{x}}^{19}}$

concepts.title: Greatest Term
concepts.conceptBody:
Greatest term in ${{(1+x)}^{n}}$ 

If ' ${{T}_{r}}$ ' and ' ${{T}_{r+1}}$ ' be the ' $r$ ' th and $\left( r+1 \right)$ the terms in the expansion of ${{(1+x)}^{n}}$, then :

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{(1)}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}$

And ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}-1}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{\text{r}-1}}$

So ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}/{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\left( \text{n}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}/{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}-1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{r}-1}} \right)=\left( \text{n}-\text{r}+1 \right)/\text{r}\left| \text{x} \right|$

If ' ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}$ be the greatest term, then ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}/{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}$

Or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}/{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=1$

Since $\left( n-r+1 \right)/r\cdot \left| x \right|>1$, where ' $r$ ' is a ' + ' ve integer.

This inequality, changes either to the form $r<m+f$ pr $r<m$, where ' $m$ ' is a ' + ' ve integer and ' $\text{f}$ ' is a fraction. So we get :

$\text{r}<\text{m}+\text{f}$……….. (i)

or $r<m$………… (ii)

In case (i), ' $\text{T}{{~}_{\text{m}+1}}$ is the greatest term, and in case (i) ' $\text{T}{{~}_{\text{m}}}$ and ' $\text{T}{{~}_{\text{m}+1}}$ are the greatest terms, and both re equal.

Short-cut : First calculate $\text{m}=\left| \text{x}\left( \text{n}+1 \right)/\left( \text{x}+1 \right) \right|$

Case (1) if ' $\text{m}$ ' is an integer, then ' $\text{T}$ ' ${{~}_{\text{m}}}$ and ' $\text{T}{{~}_{\text{m}+1}}$ are the greatest terms and both are equal.

Case (2) if ' $\text{m}$ ' is not an integer, then ${{\text{T}}_{\left[ \text{m} \right]+1}}$ will be the greatest term, where [.] denotes greatest integer function.

Find numerically the greatest term in the expansion of (2 + 3x), when x = (3 / 2) 

Solution:  Method I :-

${{(2+3x)}^{9}}={{2}^{9}}{{[1+3x/2]}^{9}}$

In the expansion of ${{[\left( 1+3x \right)/2]}^{9}}$, we have :

$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}+1} / \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}=[(9-\mathrm{r}+1) / \mathrm{r}] \mid 3 \mathrm{x} / 2$

$=((10-r) / r)|(3 / 2) \times(3 / 2)|^3$

$=(10-\mathrm{r}) / \mathrm{r} \times 9 / 4$

$\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{r}+1} / \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}=(90-9 \mathrm{r}) / 4 \mathrm{r}$

$\text { Putting } \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}+1} / \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}>1$

$\Rightarrow(90-9 \mathrm{r}) / 4 \mathrm{r}>1$

$\text { or } 90 \geq 13 \mathrm{r}$

$\text { or } \mathrm{r} \leq 90 / 13$

$\text { or } \mathrm{r} \leq 6+12 / 13$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_{6+1} \text { or }{ }^{\prime} \mathrm{T}_7{ }^{\prime} \text { is the greatest term. }$

${ }^{\prime} \mathrm{T}_7{ }^{\prime} \text { in }[1+(3 \mathrm{x} / 2)]^9$

$\mathrm{~T}_7=\mathrm{T}^{6+1}={ }^9 \mathrm{C}_6(3 \times / 6)^6$

$=9 ! /(3 ! 6 !) \cdot[(3 / 2) \times(3 / 2)]^6$

$=(9.8 .7 / 3.2 .1) \times\left(9^6 / 4^6\right)$

$=\left(3 \times 7 \times 9^6\right) / 45=\left(3 \times 7 \times 3^{12}\right) / 2^{10}$

$=7 \cdot\left(3^{13} / 2^{10}\right)$

So greatest term in ${{(2+3\text{x})}^{9}}$ is :

$~={{2}^{9}}\cdot 7\cdot \left( {{3}^{13}}/{{2}^{10}} \right)$

$~=\left( 7\times {{3}^{13}} \right)/2$

Method II :- 

${{(2+3\text{x})}^{9}}$$~={{2}^{9}}{{[\left( 1+3\text{x} \right)/2]}^{9}}$

$~={{2}^{9}}{{[1+9/4]}^{9}}$

Since x $~=3/2$

Here m $~=\left| \text{x}\left( \text{n}+1 \right)/\left( \text{x}+1 \right) \right|=(9/4\left( 9+1 \right)/9/4+1\mid $

$~=90/13$

So greatest term in the expansion is ${{\text{T}}_{\left[ \text{m} \right]+1}}={{\text{T}}_{3+1}}={{\text{T}}_{7}}$

Now the method is same as in method (1)

Greatest Coefficient: In any binomial expansion middle-term has the greatest Coefficient. So

(i) If ' $n$ ' is even, then greatest coefficient $={{~}^{n}}{{C}_{n/2}}$

(ii) If ' $\text{n}$ ' is odd, then greatest coefficients are ${{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\left( \text{n}+1 \right)/2}}$ and ${{~}^{\text{n}}}\text{C}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)/2$
